caption:
Here’s what nobody tells you about “relationship goals”: the magic isn’t in never breaking. It’s in how beautifully you rebuild. ✨

You can tell everything about a couple not by watching them at brunch, but by watching what happens when someone messes up. Do they retreat into separate corners or find their way back to each other’s humanity? ❤️

The couples who make it aren’t the ones who never disconnect. They’re the ones who’ve learned that disconnection is just the comma, not the period. Real intimacy lives in the aftermath when someone says “I was wrong” and actually means it. When you can be pissed off without making it dangerous. 

It’s a fools game to romanticize the highlight reel. The measure of love isn’t in the absence of mess, it’s in your willingness to clean it up together. Again and again. Without keeping score. 

#relationships #love #connection

caption:
Comment EFFORT for a free pdf teaching you what to do in this scenario. 
.
One of you is pushing for change, the other is holding space for what already feels okay. This isn’t incompatibility, it’s a common rhythm many couples get stuck in.

One partner feels alone in the effort to grow, the other feels pressured just for being themselves. It’s not about who’s right or wrong, but about seeing how different ways of loving show up.

If you’re the one chasing progress, try noticing the value in what’s already here without mistaking it for being stuck.

If you’re the one content with the now, take a small step toward curiosity and ask questions before the tension builds.

This dynamic isn’t a problem, it’s a chance to learn how to love across difference instead of push against it.

#relationships #growth #connection

latestComments.text: What about when you are at that four or five level and then you set a boundary to take space for 24 hours because you are feeling emotionally unsafe and completely dysregulated at work, have a meeting after work and then also have work the next day so that’s literally as long as you need to actually process the argument?! And then they take it as complete abandonment and slam the door on you and protest your boundary, accusing you of “throwing them away” and completely shutting down/threatening to end it after they already made you feel completely emotionally unsafe??? 😩😩😩 I’m at my wits end trying to make it work and feeling so hopeless and heartbroken 💔

Love you, @bayavoce - huge fan. But this sounds more like the intellectualization of a problem that happens in the body. And many people don’t struggle with understanding what to do. They (we) struggle to translate that message in a way the body can understand in realtime.
How does your degree in social work qualify you to give counseling advice? What are your qualifications?

The door slammer always feels like their trauma matters more than the other person’s and I feel like that’s what online therapy has become, is this centering of emotional neglecters and “avoidant” people because “they suffered trauma” as if they’re the only people who’ve ever suffered trauma.

caption:
The moment you stop trying to fix your partner is the moment your relationship actually starts working.

Here’s what actually shifts everything: learning to see your partner’s resistance as information, not defiance.

The couples who last aren’t the ones who never trigger each other. They’re the ones who got curious about what lives underneath the trigger instead of trying to eliminate it. They learned that your partner’s “”flaws”” are usually their survival strategies.

Your nervous system wants safety, but your soul wants growth. The magic happens when you can hold both without making your partner responsible for either.

#relationships #love #growth

latestComments.text: Well said!!
Just sent you a DM!
Often resentment towards another is used to shift blame unto another so that you dont have to face the shame and uncomfortable work of knowing you didn't let your needs be known.
Really needed this today.

She is absolutely right.

As a divorce attorney, one thing I have learned is that not all resentment is a failure to forgive.

Sometimes resentment persists because the underlying issue was never actually resolved.

Many people are told to “just let it go” without first asking whether anything has genuinely changed.

Forgiveness and reconciliation are not the same thing.

Forgiveness is an internal decision.

Trust is rebuilt through consistent behavior.

If the lies continue, the disrespect continues, the dismissiveness continues, the betrayal continues, or accountability never arrives, the mind and body often recognize what the heart is trying to ignore.

That is not always bitterness.

Sometimes it is self-protection.

Sometimes your nervous system is responding to patterns that remain unchanged.

This is why true healing requires more than apologies.

It requires accountability.

It requires behavioral change.

It requires repair.

And it requires enough consistency for trust to feel safe again.

To be balanced, not every lingering feeling means the other person is still doing something wrong. Sometimes old wounds, trauma, anxiety, or fear can keep us stuck long after the danger has passed.

That is why the real question is not:

“Why can’t I let this go?”

The real question is:

“Has anything actually changed?”

Healthy relationships are not built on repeated forgiveness alone.

They are built on accountability, safety, trust, and demonstrated change.

Follow @pepptalkwithbaa for balanced conversations on relationships, divorce, emotional intelligence, accountability, and the things many people are afraid to say out loud.

firstComment:
What if the spouse play agreeable and you are influencing each other during the three years you are dating and engaged, but then the spouse slows down once you're married - and coming to a full stop + turning 180 on you after 14 years of marriage. Then the spouse tells you what they really wants in their spouse: their spouse should serve and be quite about any inconveniences. If their spouse is not quite - their spouse is critizising them. Screaming, cursing, silent treatment. And when you had enough and say that - you are unstable and angry.

How can you check for this prior? You can't!
Many people hides the real self and can do so for a long time.

latestComments.text:

That’s so true
It requires a lot for couples and individuals to be influenced. They need to be able to put the themselves  in the other person’s shoes!!🙌
My partner wants me to delete Instagram because I care about Palestinians

Soo, when you changed and adapted to your partner's requirements for years and years but see very little or no change at all from the other side.... Though the requirements never stops. And when the relationship finally ends is because the partner that didn't change flips the blame on you and say they had enough.... So maybe the strategy should be not to change too much and then to recognize early on if your partner is changing and adjusting accordingly.... For too many men, this is a one-way Street and we find ourselves getting the blame for a failed relationship. The wife plays the victim and walk away to justify and rewrite the story.

firstComment: Great point

latestComments.text: Great point
Thank you so much for this
This is exactly me 👀 👀 👀!!😱
The Secure person acts like an Avoidants too🤔
Love it👏
Fact: the anxious one’s feelings turn out to be right most of the time

I think it's unfair to label a person as anxious when their response might be reasonable based on someone failing to act with consistency.  There are people who I cannot hear back from for days or months and I have no concerns because there are no hanging threads or they didn't disappear after an important question. There are actions and inaction that, with or without malice are destabilizing and disrespectful and are anxiety inducing even in a very secure person.  I think you have to look at the person's overall patterns: do they respond this way with every connection?  Did their responses change after a significant event?  When they are treated with respect do they still act the same?  I think placing an anxious label on someone who is responding as one should when disrespected is harmful.  It is a well known phenomenon that in situations where there is parental alienation, the parent who looks more secure and calm may not actually be the good guy: in fact they can be a controlling narcissist, while the parent experiencing genuine trauma from being alienated from their kids will naturally look more anxious : because they have been abused. Someone's response is not an indication of their issues.  It is sometimes a barometer for when something is very off in the environment.
This is so helpful!

This is such a great reflection

caption:
Being with someone who carries trauma means learning to navigate layers of experience that aren’t yours.

Their reactions are shaped by histories long before you, and your own patterns can add complexity to the dance.

True love in this context isn’t about fixing or rescuing, it’s about staying present, noticing triggers without judgment, and practicing repair that’s patient, clear, and grounded.

Here are 3 powerful questions to ask when their trauma gets activated:
1️⃣ What did this bring up in you?
2️⃣ What did this remind you of?
3️⃣ What do you need to feel safe again?

It’s asking these questions, holding space without losing yourself, and discerning between care and over-functioning. Healing happens individually, but connection deepens when both people approach it with awareness and integrity.

#relationships #healing #emotionalawareness

latestIgtvVideos.title: Rituals for big life changes
latestIgtvVideos.caption:
(Updated post) 
My work in experience design and facilitation has always followed my personal interests and questions at any given moment. I look back sweetly on this video and think about how much I was navigating and how important ritual became for me. Although this is no longer my focus, it’s still a massive piece I bring with me into the experience design I do.
.
(Old post starts here)
Ive been going through some big life changes — my engagement ending, moving cities, entering into a new relationship, shifting friend & family dynamics, health complications, etc.
.
So it goes, as life brings us new seeds to plant, so the wind carries us in new directions.
.
I’ve been designing events for the past decade. From my early days of matchmaking and singles events to the more recent immersive secret experiences (@secretslc) and women’s circles (@secretcircles_).
.
As life has continued to hand me pivotal changes, I’ve continued to set aside time to pause and create deep meaning out of these transitions via rites of passage, ritual, and ceremony.
.
As I've given more room to these practices, I've come to believe that whether we know it or not, we all crave these spaces since historically they were what took us through major life-changing events whether we were in grief or celebration.
.
I credit ritual and ceremony for turning the hardest periods of my life into rites of passage.
.
It’s time to pay it forward. The next series of experiences I’ll be creating will be adding rituals, rites of passage, and ceremonies into various events. These experiences are for people who want to add deep meaning into an event.
.
One of the things I’ve learned over my years of experience design is that when we create this kind of intentional space, these become the moments that we’ll remember in the end.
.
If this sounds like something you’d like to add into a gathering you’re hosting, please send me a DM, I'd be happy to talk about how I can help.
(Obviously there’s no button since this was made for a website so please DM!)
.
Sending love —
